About

My career didn't follow a plan. It followed curiosity.

I started in agencies — where the pace was relentless and the briefs were always changing. That taught me how to think across categories, move fast, and find what's interesting in almost any product. A luxury internship at Schbang working on Tata CLiQ taught me something different: how premium brands protect the feeling of a product, not just its features. And building Totto — a D2C brand from scratch out of a Delhi wholesale market — taught me what it actually takes to make something exist that didn't before.

Each stop added a layer. And I'm still adding.

The through-line across every role has been the same: get to the real problem before solving the visible one. Channel strategy, creative direction, distribution architecture — none of it holds if the brief underneath is wrong. I spend disproportionate time there.

I now sit at the intersection of brand thinking and growth systems. Turns out they were never really separate.

Brand Thinking
Positioning, audience understanding, emotional hooks, cultural fit. What a brand stands for — and how that becomes communication people actually remember.
Consumer Understanding
Lifecycle thinking, behavioural insight, retention systems. How people move through a brand — and where the experience quietly breaks down.
Integrated Campaigns
Social, CRM, performance, ATL/BTL, in-app, influencer — as one coordinated system, not isolated tactics that ignore each other.
Execution at Scale
Cross-functional coordination, campaign operations across agencies and internal teams, strategy that holds together under real timelines.

Brand & Growth Marketing

GTM Strategy · Swiggy Scenes · Sep – Nov 2025

Scenes Scale-Up:
From traction to mainstream

Designing a six-channel campaign architecture to achieve 5× new users and 2× weekly sessions — ₹75L, 3 months

Role
Strategy Lead
Budget Planned
₹75 Lakhs
Timeline
Sep – Nov 2025
Goal
5× NUS · 2× VU
S
Situation
Scenes had proof of concept. What it didn't have was a mainstream audience.

Swiggy Scenes had 11K+ events live, 25K+ covers sold, and a growing base of transacting users. The product worked. The problem: most Swiggy users had never heard of Scenes at all. Growth had plateaued at 3.1 lakh sessions per week, and the window to establish Scenes as India's default events platform was open — but not indefinitely.

T
Task
Design a campaign that builds awareness and habit — simultaneously.
  • 5× new user acquisition — people actively transacting on Scenes, not just aware of it
  • 2× weekly sessions — building a discovery habit from 3.1L to 6.2L
  • Brand awareness lift — before/after study to quantify recall shift

Off-app acquisition and on-app engagement had to work as one coordinated loop — not separate campaigns that don't speak to each other.

A
Action
Six pillars. One user at the centre.

User first. Before the media plan, I built the persona. Tara Shah: 23, content strategist, Bangalore. FOMO-driven, Insta-first. Pain points: too many options, no clear signal, same venues every weekend. Every creative and targeting decision ran through her.

Influencer strategy. 30+ creators across macro, micro, and nano tiers. Selection filter: city-relevant creators whose audiences were already attending events — not generic lifestyle influencers. Activations included Scenes event attendance content, day-in-the-life formats centred around weekend experiences, and Q&A-format discovery content.

Performance layer. Retargeting audiences built from Swiggy's existing user base — lookalikes from transacting Scenes users, interest signals around live music, comedy, fitness, dining. Performance budget weighted toward retargeting over acquisition, with creative testing running weekly.

CRM integration. In-app push, email, and WhatsApp sequences designed around the event browse-to-book journey. Personalisation by city, event category, and past behaviour. Abandoned browse recovery and post-event follow-up built into the flow.

Budget Allocation — ₹75L Across 6 Channels
ChannelAllocationPrimary Goal
Influencer (30+ creators)₹25LAwareness + social proof
Meta + Google Performance₹20LNUS acquisition
Spotify Audio/Video₹10LBrand recall, city-level awareness
CRM (push, email, WhatsApp)₹8LSession frequency + retention
OOH (select cities)₹8LCultural presence, credibility
Brand Awareness Study₹4LBefore/after measurement
R
Result
A fully funded, cross-channel plan with measurable targets at every stage.
₹75LTotal Budget Planned
30+Influencers Activated
6Channels Working as One
5×NUS Target Set
Co-Brand Partnership · HDFC Bank × Swiggy Scenes · 2025

Co-brand partnership
with HDFC Bank and Swiggy Scenes

Building the framework, audience pitch, and offer structure that connected a financial brand with Gen Z through an events platform

Role
Partnership Strategist
Partner
HDFC Bank
Category
Co-Brand Partnership
Offer
10% off · HDFC card
S
Situation
HDFC wanted to reach Gen Z. The standard playbook wasn't working.

India's Gen Z population — 377 million strong — was the most valuable emerging financial segment for HDFC Bank. But standard banking communications weren't landing with them. The problem wasn't reach. It was context: financial advertising shows up in the wrong moments. The brief was to find a channel where HDFC could be present when Gen Z was already in a spending mindset — not interrupting their day, but fitting into it.

T
Task
Build a partnership pitch with a clear business case on both sides.
  • Make the case for Swiggy Scenes as a Gen Z acquisition channel — not just a sponsorship vehicle
  • Design an offer structure that drives actual card usage, not just brand visibility
  • Build a co-branding framework that could scale across 11K+ events without diluting either brand
A
Action
Lead with their objective, not our inventory.

The pitch was structured around HDFC's problem, not Scenes' product. The data: 377M Gen Z in India. 25K+ covers sold on Scenes — a premium, captive audience already mid-transaction. The framing wasn't "sponsor our events." It was "here is a channel where your customer already has their card out."

Offer Structure — Designed for Behaviour, Not Just Discount
ParameterValueRationale
Discount10% offMeaningful enough to drive decision; not so high it erodes event margin
Max Cap₹500Keeps unit economics commercially viable for HDFC
Min Order Value₹2,500Matches premium event ticket pricing naturally — doesn't feel forced
TriggerHDFC card at checkoutDrives measurable card usage, not just brand awareness
R
Result
Partnership live across 11K+ events. A new model for brand-event co-marketing.
  • HDFC in-app co-branding and onground presence activated across Scenes events
  • HDFC visible across 11K+ Scenes events — each a contextual, low-cost brand touchpoint
  • Offer live: HDFC cardholders receive 10% off on select Scenes events
  • Partnership demonstrated events as a high-quality Gen Z acquisition channel for financial brands
377MGen Z in India — core pitch anchor
11K+Events with HDFC brand presence
25K+Covers — captive premium audience
10%Offer structure designed and deployed
Event Branding · Experiential · Swiggy Scenes

Shuffle Showdown:
Onground brand system for a pickleball event

The brief was venue branding. The actual work was mapping the evening — then building Scenes into every moment people would naturally stop and look

Role
Event Marketing Lead
Format
Sports + Social Event
Focus
Onground Brand System
Goal
Brand Recall + UGC
S
Situation
Pickleball had become a social occasion. Scenes needed a presence in that space.

By 2024, pickleball had crossed from sport into social event — the kind of evening young urban professionals were turning up to, photographing, and talking about. Scenes was building a set of owned cultural IPs: Fake Shaadi, Coffee Rave, Bhag Club. Shuffle Showdown was the sports-social edition. The challenge wasn't the event itself — it was making the venue feel like a Scenes property, not a court with some logos on it.

T
Task
Map the attendee journey. Find the moments. Build Scenes into each one.
  • Design a complete brand system across the full venue
  • Identify which moments in the evening have the highest photograph potential
  • Deliver all elements at production-ready specifications, on time
A
Action
Six touchpoints. Each placed where the evening naturally pauses.
Brand Touchpoint Map — Six Moments, Six Jobs
TouchpointBrand JobUGC Potential
Entrance ArchFirst impression · 100% of attendees walk throughVery High
Court BrandingContinuous brand visibility during playHigh
Trophy + MedalsWinners post organically — branded proof of winVery High
Photo WallDedicated UGC moment — designed to be postedVery High
F&B Zone BrandingDwell time · relaxed brand exposureMedium
MerchandisePost-event brand recall + organic reachHigh
R
Result
Six branded touchpoints. 100% of attendees walked through the arch. Organic content generated.
  • Complete brand system designed and delivered on production-ready specifications
  • Entrance arch ensured 100% of attendees had a Scenes-branded first moment
  • Photo wall and trophy design drove organic UGC from attendees post-event
  • Event established Scenes as a credible player in the sports-social category
6+Brand touchpoints designed and delivered
360°Full attendee journey mapped
100%Attendees through branded arch
UGCOrganic content generated post-event
CRO & Data Analysis · FMCG · 2024

Bombay Shaving Company:
Finding the real drop-off

BSC's conversion rate had been dropping for a month. The tech integration was blamed. I mapped 30 days of funnel data and found the real origin — it wasn't where anyone expected.

Role
Data Analyst / CRO
Brand
Bombay Shaving Co.
Duration
30-day analysis
Outcome
4 Prioritised Fixes
S
Situation
CR had been dropping for a month. Everyone blamed the tech integration. I wasn't so sure.

Bombay Shaving Company's website conversion rate had been declining steadily since late July. The internal assumption — shared across the team — was that a recent third-party tech integration was responsible. Before rebuilding or removing it, a structured data review was needed. The question: is the integration actually the problem?

T
Task
Map 30 days of funnel data. Find the real source. Don't guess.
  • Phase the CR decline: when exactly did it start, and what changed at that moment?
  • Identify the primary drop-off stage in the funnel — not the lowest point, but the largest loss
  • Confirm or clear the tech integration as the cause
  • Deliver a prioritised action plan tied to specific stages
A
Action
Phase the data. Find the shape. The shape tells you where to look.

Pulled 30 days of GA4 data and broke it into four distinct phases rather than reading it as one aggregate. The shape of the CR curve across phases told a different story than the flat average: the decline didn't start when the integration went live.

Sessions
100%
Product Views
68%
Add to Cart
10.2%
Checkout Initiated
6.4%
Purchase
0.83%

The largest drop was at Add to Cart — 10.2%. 90% of visitors left before showing any purchase intent. This isn't a checkout problem or a payment problem. It's a product page conviction problem.

Prioritised Fix 1: Product page
P1
Prioritised Fix 2: SEO intent keywords
P2
Prioritised Fix 3: Checkout friction
P3
Prioritised Fix 4: Cart recovery
P4
R
Result
Integration cleared. Root cause found. Full roadmap delivered.
  • Confirmed the tech integration was not responsible — protecting the client relationship and redirecting effort correctly
  • Identified 23 July as the exact start of the decline, mapped across four distinct phases
  • Located the primary drop-off: Add to Cart at 10.2% — a product page conviction problem, not a checkout issue
  • Delivered a prioritised, timeline-based action plan covering product page, SEO, checkout, and cart recovery
0.83%CR low point — identified and dated
10.2%Add to Cart — where users actually left
23 JulyExact date decline began
4Prioritised fixes delivered
D2C Brand Build · Masters' Union 2024

Totto:
Building a brand from a wholesale market in Delhi

No budget, no audience, no supplier. A market hypothesis and three months to make it real.

Role
Founder
Category
Women's Handbags
Timeline
3 months
Context
Masters' Union Challenge
S
Situation
Build a real business from zero. No theoretical answers.

The Masters' Union D2C Challenge (April 2024) required building a D2C business end-to-end — market research, brand identity, supplier sourcing, distribution, and live sales — inside a defined sprint. The Indian handbag market was projected at US$4.5bn in 2024, online-underpenetrated with room for a new brand positioned correctly.

T
Task
Find the right niche. Build the brand. Open distribution. Generate revenue.
  • Identify which subcategory had real demand and manageable entry friction
  • Build a brand identity with enough personality to be remembered
  • Source a reliable supplier and build multiple distribution channels
  • Generate real revenue within three months
A
Action
Research the market in person. Then build everything around what you actually found.

Ground-up category research. Before building anything, I spent time in Delhi's wholesale bag markets — Nabi Karim — talking to sellers, watching what was moving, listening to what buyers asked for. Started with a broad mix: office bags, sling bags, shoulder bags. The market told me what to narrow to: party clutches and bridal bags. Higher emotional purchase intent, faster turnover, less competition from generic sellers. That pivot was made before a single rupee was spent on inventory.

Full brand built in-house. I designed and executed the entire Totto brand identity myself — no agency, no outsourcing.

  • Brand name: "Totto" from "in toto" — completeness. Totto bags complete your look.
  • Logo: Designed a clean wordmark with a subtle handbag element in the letter T
  • Thank-you cards: Designed and printed physical cards with a QR code linking to the Instagram page — placed inside every order. Packaging as a digital acquisition channel.
  • Instagram content: Shot and edited all product content. Used original audio in Reels instead of trending sounds — a deliberate choice to stand out rather than blend in.
Sales & Distribution — Three Channels, All Built from Scratch
ChannelWhat I DidResult
Instagram (@tottobags)Original-audio Reels, high-quality product shoots, storytelling captions, Stories with UGC stickers — all content created and shot in-house.5,700 accounts reached · 836 profile visits · 10,495 impressions · inbound DMs from buyers
Swiggy MinisListed products for in-city fast delivery. Connected Facebook Pixel to the store for future targeting.Orders fulfilled; tapped Swiggy's existing discovery infrastructure
Exhibitions & EventsParticipated in fashion markets and local exhibitions — direct customer interaction, in-person brand building, and physical touchpoints that converted on the day.New customer acquisition; word-of-mouth referrals within weeks of the first exhibition
Brand Identity & Social Content
Instagram reach · Jan 26 – Apr 24 · 5,700 accounts · 10,495 impressions
Top content · Reels reaching 1,852 accounts at peak
Profile activity · 836 profile visits · 14 external link taps
Inbound customer DM — reached via Reel, asked for price

Financial performance. Each bag was sourced at an average raw material cost of ₹765 per unit and sold at ₹1,200 — a deliberate margin structure that had to absorb packaging, transport, and platform costs too. 10K+ impressions organically. ₹1.2L in revenue with zero paid spend.

R
Result
₹1.2L revenue. 10K+ impressions. 3 channels. Built from zero, entirely organically.
₹1.2LRevenue Generated
10K+Impressions Organically
5,700Instagram Accounts Reached

₹1.2L in revenue acquired entirely organically across 3 distribution channels — Instagram DMs, college exhibitions, and peer referral. Sales peaked in February–March 2024 during wedding season — the party-clutch pivot, made after the Nabi Karim market visit, was the call that shaped everything. Inbound DMs from people who'd heard about Totto from existing buyers started coming in within weeks of launch.

Each item was sourced at an average raw material cost of ₹765 per unit — leaving meaningful room for margin at the ₹1,200 price point. The business reached ₹1.2L in revenue with no external funding, no paid ads, and no prior brand equity.

Learnings
01
If the price only covers raw material, the business is already losing
Early pricing only covered inventory cost — which looked profitable until you added packaging, transport, platform fees, and time. Real margin only appeared once the price absorbed all of that. Pricing off raw material cost is how small businesses stay small. The number on the tag has to carry the whole operation.
02
A big market is a trap if you try to serve all of it from day one
The Indian handbag market is enormous — which made it easy to spread thin and hard to stand out. The move that worked was focusing on one occasion (gifting) in one channel (exhibitions) until it converted consistently. Niching down felt like shrinking the opportunity. It was actually how you build one that's defensible before you diversify.
03
Repeat purchase is the only honest signal of product-market fit
50+ customers were acquired organically — but repeat purchase rate was the real signal. The SKUs that generated repeat interest were occasion bags at a clear price point. The ones that didn't move pointed to a fit problem, not a marketing problem. No amount of content fixes a product people don't come back for.
B2B Consulting · Fragrance · Dubai · Entry in $1 Perfume Market · 2024

Launching Pars Heaven into Dubai's
$4.6Bn fragrance market

A brand-new affordable-luxury perfume business in Deira needed a complete go-to-market. I built a 4-phase B2B launch strategy covering brand positioning, pre-launch buzz, retailer partnerships, and an omnichannel campaign — targeting first shipment by October 2024.

Role
Brand & Growth Strategist
Context
Entry in $1 Perfume Market
Market
UAE · MENA · B2B
S
Situation
A new perfume brand in Deira. A $4.6Bn market. No awareness, no distribution, no launch plan.

Pars Heaven Perfumes Trading LLC is based in Deira, Dubai — the traditional heart of the UAE's fragrance trade. The brand targets the affordable-luxury segment (the "$1 perfume" market) and operates B2B, supplying retailers and distributors across MENA and beyond. Their initial retail partner was Lulu, one of the region's largest hypermarket chains.

Despite a clear product and an established retail anchor, the brand had no go-to-market strategy. The Dubai fragrance market is intensely competitive — dominated by heritage luxury houses at the top and high-volume commodity brands at the bottom. Breaking in as a new B2B supplier required more than a product; it required a brand story, a buzz strategy, and a sequenced retailer expansion plan. The first shipment needed to land by October 2024.

T
Task
Design a full B2B launch strategy — from brand identity to retailer engagement to first shipment.
  • Define Pars Heaven's unique value proposition in the affordable-luxury fragrance segment
  • Build a pre-launch buzz strategy using influencers, KOLs, and targeted outreach
  • Map B2B channel opportunities — both digital (directories, marketplaces) and physical (retailers, trade shows)
  • Design a 4-phase launch roadmap with the omnichannel campaign as the final activation
  • Identify guerrilla and community tactics to create organic awareness in Dubai's fragrance community
A
Action
Four phases. One goal: get retailers stocking and customers talking before the first shipment arrives.
4-Phase Launch Strategy
PhaseFocusKey Actions
01 — Brand Positioning & AwarenessDefine the brand in the $1 perfume marketUVP development; elegant packaging design; website & marketing assets; brand storytelling; social media teasers + influencer partnerships
02 — Pre-Launch Buzz GenerationBuild anticipation before product hits shelvesIdentify KOLs in Dubai's fragrance circles; influencer content collabs; targeted email campaign to curated retailer/distributor list
03 — Retailer Engagement & PartnershipsSecure distribution through high-end retailOutreach to Bloomingdale's, Saks Fifth Avenue, Harvey Nichols; prime in-store placements; retailer-exclusive SKUs; staff training programme
04 — Omnichannel Launch CampaignActivate awareness at scaleSocial media ads targeting domestic + international retailers; OOH in luxury malls; lifestyle media partnerships; exclusive launch event; limited-time launch promotion

B2B digital presence. Mapped 7 platforms for building Pars Heaven's B2B brand awareness and connecting with international retailers and distributors: Alibaba.com (global reach), Biznex.ae (UAE-specific B2B), DubaiExporters.com (export-focused trade), TradersFind (UAE B2B portal), TradeFord.com (global manufacturer-buyer connections), UAE Business Directory (local credibility), and HiDubai.com (B2B/B2C directory).

Network-building strategy. Recommended active community presence across: Dubai Chamber of Commerce, Fragrance Foundation Middle East, Beautyworld Middle East trade fair, Dubai Shopping Festival pop-ups, LinkedIn groups in fragrance/cosmetics, and local hubs like Dubai Startup Hub and BNI chapters.

Marketing funnel design. Built a 4-layer marketing strategy: Content Marketing (top of funnel — fragrance storytelling, editorial content) → Website & Identity Development (brand credibility, multilingual, SEO-optimised, GMB listing) → Network Marketing (community-led growth via directories, trade events, B2B outreach) → Paid Ads (targeted at domestic and international B2B retailers via social + DOOH).

Buzz and guerrilla tactics. Designed a pre-launch buzz playbook including: soft launch with influencer seeding; influencer and KOL collaborations in beauty and fragrance; DOOH ads at Dubai airports and luxury mall corridors; hotel partnerships with 4-star properties (Novotel, Mercure, ibis) for international retailer attendees; press release distribution to local beauty media; and guerrilla activations — scent bombing in high-traffic areas, fragrance flashmobs, sampling stunts outside competitor stores, and fragrance-themed social media giveaways.

R
Result
A complete B2B launch playbook — 4 phases, 7 digital channels, a retailer outreach strategy, and a first-shipment target of October 2024.
4Launch Phases Designed
7B2B Digital Channels Mapped

$4.6BnUAE Fragrance Market Size

Delivered a full consultancy deck covering market context, SWOT, 4-phase launch strategy, B2B channel map, marketing funnel, network strategy, and buzz creation tactics. The recommendation prioritised brand-building and B2B directory presence before paid advertising — establishing awareness infrastructure that would support the Lulu partnership and future retail expansion across MENA.

Learnings
01
B2B and B2C are completely different systems — what works in one breaks in the other
Consumer marketing logic — awareness, reach, social proof — doesn't translate to B2B. Distributors and retailers don't browse Instagram to find suppliers. They look at trade directories, ask for referrals within their network, and evaluate credibility through associations and certifications. The acquisition funnel is relationship-first, not content-first. Building it requires patience and a completely different playbook.
02
Trade shows and B2B events are where the actual business gets done
In B2B fragrance, Beautyworld Middle East and regional trade events are where distributors actively look for new brands to stock. These aren't optional marketing expenses — they're the primary channel where buyers seek suppliers. A brand that skips trade shows in this market is invisible to the decision-makers who actually hold the distribution keys.
03
In a mostly offline market, having a real online identity makes you easy to find and hard to ignore
Most fragrance traders in Deira operate without a structured digital identity — no website, no consistent brand presence, no searchable credibility. For Pars Heaven, building that digital layer wasn't about driving online sales. It was about standing out from hundreds of similar suppliers when a potential buyer Googled the brand. In a market where everyone looks the same offline, your online presence is what makes you findable and memorable.
